title: Housing in-lamb ewes before lambing
subtitle: What they need indoors, and when to bring them in.
excerpt: Ewes do not need to come in months before lambing and many small flocks lamb outdoors. But first-time keepers and those expecting twins or triplets will find that housing for the final 4 to 6 weeks of pregnancy gives real advantages. Space requirements, feed changes, daily checks, and the signs that labour is starting.
sourceNotes: DEFRA/APHA Code of Practice for the Welfare of Sheep (2021). AHDB Beef and Lamb, ewe management through pregnancy guide (2023). John Seymour, The New Complete Book of Self-Sufficiency (2003), sheep chapter. SAC Consulting Scotland, sheep housing and pre-lambing management.

glossaryTerms.definition: A ewe that is pregnant. Confirmed by scanning (ultrasound) around day 70 of the 147-day gestation period, which reveals how many foetuses she is carrying.

Also called pregnancy toxaemia. A metabolic disorder in late pregnancy when the energy demand of multiple foetuses outstrips the ewe's intake. Ewes show progressive dullness, grinding teeth, and collapse. Prevention is better than cure: avoid condition score collapse in the last 6 weeks of pregnancy.

Minerals including selenium, copper, cobalt, and iodine that sheep need in small amounts for immunity, growth, and reproductive function. Deficiency is common on some soil types and may require supplementation in late pregnancy.

The first milk produced by a ewe after lambing, rich in antibodies and energy. Lambs must suckle within the first 2 hours of life to absorb these antibodies; the gut closes to passive transfer within hours of birth.

content.text: Bringing
in-lamb ewes

indoors 4 to 6 weeks before the first due date gives you control over their feed, a warmer environment for early spring lambing, and the ability to monitor closely as labour approaches. It is not mandatory: many hardy breeds lamb perfectly well outdoors in good weather. But for first-time keepers, or for ewes carrying twins or triplets where the risk of
twin lamb disease
is higher, housing reduces the hard cases.
When to bring them in

Aim to house ewes 4 to 6 weeks before the expected lambing window. Housing them earlier puts them under unnecessary pressure indoors; housing them with less than 4 weeks to go does not give you enough time to adjust their nutrition properly. If you scanned for twins and triplets, house those animals first. Single-bearing ewes can lamb outdoors with less intervention risk.
Space requirements

Allow at least 1.5 m² per ewe for commercial breeds, and 1.2 m² for smaller breeds. Overcrowding causes fighting at the trough, increases disease pressure, and stresses the ewes in late pregnancy. Pen singles and twins separately from triplet-bearing ewes if space allows; triplet ewes need more feed and closer attention.

The shed must have adequate ventilation without draught at floor level. Ammonia build-up from urine damages lung tissue in both ewes and newborn lambs. Air should move, but no cold wind should blow across the bedded area.
Bedding and mucking out

Deep straw bedding gives ewes insulation, cushioning for heavily pregnant bodies, and something to stand on that is not a hard wet floor. Top up with fresh straw daily; the deep litter builds up slowly and can be removed after lambing. A thin wet surface is not acceptable: ewes spend much of the day lying down and a wet lying area in late pregnancy increases the risk of mastitis and navel infection at birth.
Feeding the in-lamb ewe indoors

In the last 6 weeks of pregnancy, the foetuses grow rapidly and compress the ewe's rumen, reducing her capacity for bulk feed. She cannot eat enough hay alone to meet her energy needs. Supplement with a small amount of concentrate feed (roughly 0.3 to 0.5 kg per head per day for a twin-bearing ewe, rising to 0.7 kg for triplets) split into two feeds. Increase gradually over 2 weeks to avoid a sudden dietary shift triggering acidosis.
Fresh water must always be available. A ewe in late pregnancy drinking well is eating well; a ewe not drinking is a welfare concern. Check
trace elements
with your vet if your soil type is known to be deficient; selenium and copper deficiencies affect lamb viability and ewe condition at lambing.
Daily checks

Walk through the pen twice daily in the final 2 weeks. What you are looking for: any ewe that is separated from the group, any ewe lying and not rising when you move around her, grinding teeth (a sign of pain or metabolic distress), and discharge from the vulva. A small amount of clear mucus is normal in the last week; a heavy yellow or greenish discharge is not. Call your vet if discharge is coloured.

attrs.title: Signs that labour is starting
attrs.body: The udder fills in the last 48 hours; it will feel tight and warm. The ewe starts to separate from the group and paw the bedding. She may lie down and rise repeatedly. Visible straining and a water bag at the vulva mean active labour has begun. First and second stage labour together should not exceed 2 hours in a normal presentation. If a ewe has been straining for more than 45 minutes without a water bag appearing, investigate.

content.text: At lambing

Once the lamb is delivered, clear the mucus from its mouth and nose and spray the navel with iodine solution within the first 10 minutes. The navel stump is the main entry point for joint ill and navel ill bacteria in the first days of life. Ensure the lamb stands and suckles within the first hour;
colostrum
intake in the first 2 hours of life determines its survival odds.

items.symptom: Ewe going off her feet in late pregnancy
items.cause: Twin lamb disease (pregnancy toxaemia): energy intake too low for the number of foetuses she is carrying
items.fix: Drench with a proprietary propylene glycol preparation twice daily as directed on the label. Separate from the group, offer concentrate feed little and often. Call your vet if she is not responsive within 24 hours.

items.symptom: Ewes fighting at the feed trough
items.cause: Overstocking, or a trough with insufficient space for the number of animals
items.fix: Allow 45 to 55 cm of trough space per ewe for concentrate feeding. Feed simultaneously from both sides if using a central trough. Separate any particularly aggressive individuals.

items.symptom: Ammonia smell strong in the shed
items.cause: Thin bedding, poor ventilation, or manure building up faster than fresh straw is added
items.fix: Add a deep layer of fresh straw immediately. Check ridge ventilation or open side vents. A well-managed deep litter bed does not smell strongly. Persistent ammonia means the bedding or ventilation needs attention.
